title: Negotiating a Business Contract
summary: Two business partners negotiate the terms of a supply agreement, balancing price, delivery schedules, and risk.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: Thanks for making the trip, Marcus. I know Sydney to New York isn't exactly a short flight.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: Happy to do it. I think there's a real opportunity here if we can align on the key terms.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: Agreed. Let's start with volume. Our modelling suggests we can move around forty thousand units per quarter, but only if the unit price comes down to fourteen dollars.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: Fourteen is tight for us. Our current cost structure makes sixteen the floor, and that's before factoring in freight.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: What if we commit to a minimum order of a hundred and sixty thousand units annually? Economies of scale should give you some room.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: A firm annual commitment does change the maths. We could consider fifteen-fifty with a tiered rebate once you clear two hundred thousand.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: I can live with fifteen-fifty provided the rebate kicks in at one-ninety rather than two hundred. Our forecast has us right at that boundary.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: One-ninety is workable. Now, what about payment terms? Sixty days net is standard for us.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: We'd prefer forty-five days. Cash flow is tighter here given our seasonal peaks in March and October.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: We can do forty-five if you put down a ten percent deposit on each purchase order. That covers our working capital exposure.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: Fair enough. Deposits are manageable. The bigger issue for me is liability in transit. Last year we had a shipment arrive thirty percent damaged.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: We use a bonded carrier with full insurance up to the port of destination. Beyond that, risk transfers to your logistics provider.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: We'd need that extended to our warehouse door. I can share our preferred insurer if that helps streamline the process.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: Let me check with our legal team, but in principle that sounds reasonable. What's your position on exclusivity?

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: We'd like exclusivity in the Asia-Pacific region for twenty-four months. After that, performance clauses would apply.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: Twenty-four months is a long window. Twelve months with a renewal option tied to hitting ninety percent of forecast targets feels more balanced.

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: Eighteen months, automatic renewal if we're within five percent of target, and a right of first refusal if you bring on a second Asia-Pacific partner.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: That's a reasonable middle ground. I think we're close to a term sheet. Shall we get both legal teams on a call this week?

body.speaker: Partner1
body.text: Absolutely. I'll send a summary of what we've agreed so we're both working from the same document.

body.speaker: Partner2
body.text: Perfect. It's been a productive morning. I think this partnership has real legs.

annotations.meaningVi: Using data and calculations to predict a future outcome.
annotations.exampleEn: Our modelling suggests strong demand next quarter.

annotations.meaningVi: The cost charged per single item sold.
annotations.exampleEn: If the unit price drops, we can order more.

annotations.meaningVi: The breakdown of all expenses involved in producing or delivering a product.
annotations.exampleEn: A lean cost structure keeps margins healthy.

annotations.meaningVi: The lowest price a seller is willing to accept.
annotations.exampleEn: Sixteen dollars is the absolute floor for us.

annotations.meaningVi: Including something as part of a calculation or plan.
annotations.exampleEn: We need to factor in freight costs.

annotations.meaningVi: Cost advantages gained by increasing the volume of production.
annotations.exampleEn: Larger orders benefit from economies of scale.

annotations.meaningVi: A discount that increases as purchase volume passes set thresholds.
annotations.exampleEn: A tiered rebate rewards our best customers.

annotations.meaningVi: To exceed or surpass a given quantity or threshold.
annotations.exampleEn: Once you clear two hundred thousand units, the rebate applies.

annotations.meaningVi: Payment is due within the specified number of days (e.g., 'net 60' = pay within 60 days).
annotations.exampleEn: Our standard terms are sixty days net.

annotations.meaningVi: The movement of money in and out of a business.
annotations.exampleEn: Seasonal sales create cash flow challenges.

annotations.meaningVi: Times of the year when demand and revenue are highest.
annotations.exampleEn: March and October are our seasonal peaks.

annotations.meaningVi: A formal document sent by a buyer to a seller to authorize a purchase.
annotations.exampleEn: A deposit is required with each purchase order.

annotations.meaningVi: The risk that a company's short-term assets won't cover short-term liabilities.
annotations.exampleEn: Deposits reduce our working capital exposure.

annotations.meaningVi: A licensed freight company that is financially guaranteed to cover losses or damage.
annotations.exampleEn: We always ship with a bonded carrier for insurance.

annotations.meaningVi: The harbour where goods are delivered at the end of a sea voyage.
annotations.exampleEn: Risk transfers at the port of destination.

annotations.meaningVi: A company that manages the transportation and storage of goods.
annotations.exampleEn: Our logistics provider handles last-mile delivery.

annotations.meaningVi: The right to be the only buyer or seller in a given market.
annotations.exampleEn: Exclusivity prevents competitors from entering the region.

annotations.meaningVi: A contract condition that requires a party to meet specified targets.
annotations.exampleEn: Performance clauses ensure both sides stay committed.

annotations.meaningVi: The contractual right to match any offer before it is accepted by another party.
annotations.exampleEn: A right of first refusal protects our regional position.

annotations.meaningVi: A non-binding document outlining the main points of a potential agreement.
annotations.exampleEn: A term sheet is the first step before a full contract.

annotations.meaningVi: To reach agreement or a shared understanding about something.
annotations.exampleEn: We need to align on pricing before moving forward.

annotations.meaningVi: To make a process simpler and more efficient.
annotations.exampleEn: Sharing our insurer's details will streamline the paperwork.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional structures in negotiation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Business negotiation frequently uses conditional sentences to propose trade-offs. 'If we commit to a minimum order… economies of scale should give you room' uses a real conditional (if + present simple, result with 'should'). 'We can do forty-five if you put down a deposit' uses an embedded conditional in a statement of agreement. These structures let speakers make offers contingent on the other party's actions without sounding like ultimatums. Examples: What if we commit to a minimum order of a hundred and sixty thousand units annually? / We can do forty-five if you put down a ten percent deposit on each purchase order.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Business negotiation frequently uses conditional sentences to propose trade-offs. 'If we commit to a minimum order… economies of scale should give you room' uses a real conditional (if + present simple, result with 'should'). 'We can do forty-five if you put down a deposit' uses an embedded conditional in a statement of agreement. These structures let speakers make offers contingent on the other party's actions without sounding like ultimatums. Examples: What if we commit to a minimum order of a hundred and sixty thousand units annually? / We can do forty-five if you put down a ten percent deposit on each purchase order.

grammarNotes.title: Hedging with modal verbs
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Speakers use modal verbs (could, would, can) to soften proposals and keep the negotiation collaborative. 'We could consider fifteen-fifty' is less absolute than 'We will accept fifteen-fifty.' 'We'd need that extended' uses the contracted form of 'would', signalling a preference rather than a demand. Choosing the right modal shifts the tone from confrontation to problem-solving. Examples: We could consider fifteen-fifty with a tiered rebate once you clear two hundred thousand. / We'd need that extended to our warehouse door.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Speakers use modal verbs (could, would, can) to soften proposals and keep the negotiation collaborative. 'We could consider fifteen-fifty' is less absolute than 'We will accept fifteen-fifty.' 'We'd need that extended' uses the contracted form of 'would', signalling a preference rather than a demand. Choosing the right modal shifts the tone from confrontation to problem-solving. Examples: We could consider fifteen-fifty with a tiered rebate once you clear two hundred thousand. / We'd need that extended to our warehouse door.

questions.prompt: Where is Partner1 (the female speaker) based?
questions.explanation: She thanks Marcus for travelling 'Sydney to New York', implying she is in Sydney.
questions.hint: Listen to the opening line about the flight.

questions.prompt: What unit price does Partner1 initially request?
questions.explanation: She says the price needs to 'come down to fourteen dollars'.
questions.hint: Listen for the first time a dollar figure is mentioned.

questions.prompt: What does Partner2 identify as the minimum (floor) unit price?
questions.explanation: Partner2 says 'sixteen is the floor' before freight.

questions.prompt: What annual minimum order does Partner1 propose to justify a lower price?
questions.explanation: She proposes 'a minimum order of a hundred and sixty thousand units annually'.
questions.hint: Listen for the annual commitment figure.

questions.prompt: At what volume threshold does the tiered rebate proposed by Partner2 activate?
questions.explanation: Partner2 offers a rebate 'once you clear two hundred thousand'.
questions.hint: Listen for the rebate threshold Partner2 proposes.

questions.prompt: At what threshold does Partner1 want the rebate to activate instead?
questions.explanation: She counters: 'provided the rebate kicks in at one-ninety rather than two hundred'.
questions.hint: Listen for Partner1's counter-proposal on the rebate.

questions.prompt: What are Partner2's standard payment terms?
questions.explanation: Partner2 states 'sixty days net is standard for us'.

questions.prompt: Why does Partner1 want shorter payment terms?
options: Her cash flow has seasonal peaks
questions.explanation: She explains 'cash flow is tighter here given our seasonal peaks in March and October'.
questions.hint: Listen for her explanation after stating the preferred payment period.

questions.prompt: What does Partner2 require to agree to 45-day payment terms?
questions.explanation: Partner2 says 'put down a ten percent deposit on each purchase order'.
questions.hint: Listen for the condition Partner2 attaches to the 45-day offer.

questions.prompt: What problem does Partner1 raise about transit?
options: A shipment arrived 30% damaged last year
questions.explanation: She says 'last year we had a shipment arrive thirty percent damaged'.
questions.hint: Listen for Partner1's complaint about a past shipment.

questions.prompt: Up to what point does Partner2's insurer cover transit damage?
questions.explanation: Partner2 states coverage is 'up to the port of destination'.
questions.hint: Listen for where Partner2's insurance coverage ends.

questions.prompt: What extension to insurance coverage does Partner1 request?
questions.explanation: Partner1 says 'we'd need that extended to our warehouse door'.
questions.hint: Listen for Partner1's preferred endpoint for insurance.

questions.prompt: How long does Partner1 initially want exclusivity in Asia-Pacific?
questions.explanation: She requests 'exclusivity in the Asia-Pacific region for twenty-four months'.
questions.hint: Listen for the duration Partner1 first proposes.

questions.prompt: What counter-duration does Partner2 suggest for exclusivity?
questions.explanation: Partner2 proposes 'twelve months with a renewal option'.
questions.hint: Listen for Partner2's counter-proposal on exclusivity length.

questions.prompt: What exclusivity duration do they ultimately agree on?
questions.explanation: Partner1 proposes eighteen months and Partner2 calls it 'a reasonable middle ground'.
questions.hint: Listen for the final figure Partner1 proposes and Partner2's response.

questions.prompt: What is a 'right of first refusal' as used in this conversation?
options: The right to reject any partner
The right to match any offer before another partner is brought on
The right to cancel the contract at any time
The right to set the price unilaterally
questions.explanation: Partner1 explains it as a protective right if 'you bring on a second Asia-Pacific partner'.
questions.hint: Listen to Partner1's final negotiating point on exclusivity.

questions.prompt: What do they agree to do as a next step?
options: Get both legal teams on a call
Send a shipment as a trial
questions.explanation: Partner2 says 'Shall we get both legal teams on a call this week?' and Partner1 agrees.
questions.hint: Listen for the action item at the end of the dialogue.

cloze.template: Partner1 opened by noting the journey from {{b1}} to New York before raising the first issue: volume and {{b2}}. She said their modelling suggested forty thousand units per quarter if the price reached {{b3}} dollars. Partner2 replied that their {{b4}} made sixteen the floor. Partner1 countered with a {{b5}} order of a hundred and sixty thousand units annually to unlock better pricing. Partner2 offered fifteen-fifty with a {{b6}} rebate above two hundred thousand units. Partner1 accepted fifteen-fifty but asked the rebate to start at {{b7}}. They then moved to {{b8}} terms, with Partner2's standard being sixty days {{b9}}. Partner1 preferred {{b10}} days due to seasonal cash flow peaks. Partner2 agreed on the condition of a ten percent {{b11}} per purchase order. Partner1 then raised the issue of {{b12}} liability, citing a shipment that arrived thirty percent damaged. Partner2 confirmed insurance coverage up to the {{b13}} of destination. Partner1 requested coverage extended to her {{b14}} door. Finally they discussed {{b15}} for the Asia-Pacific region, settling on {{b16}} months with automatic renewal and a right of first refusal. They agreed to convene both {{b17}} teams by the end of the week.

blanks.explanation: The Australian city Partner1 is based in.
The variable Partner1 needed the supplier to reduce.
The target unit price Partner1 requested.
The internal expense breakdown Partner2 cited.
The type of order commitment Partner1 proposed.
The type of rebate that increases with volume.
The lower threshold Partner1 negotiated for the rebate.
The word that follows the number of days in standard payment terms.
The shorter payment period Partner1 preferred.
The upfront payment per purchase order Partner2 required.
The stage during which damage occurred last year.
The location where Partner2's insurance coverage ended.
The location Partner1 wanted coverage extended to.
The right to be the sole regional partner.
The agreed duration for the exclusivity period.
The teams whose call was scheduled as the next step.

translationVi: Hai đối tác kinh doanh — một phụ nữ người Úc và một người đàn ông người Mỹ — đàm phán các điều khoản chính của thỏa thuận cung ứng, bao gồm giá đơn vị, số lượng tối thiểu, chiết khấu theo bậc, điều khoản thanh toán, phạm vi bảo hiểm vận chuyển và thời gian độc quyền tại khu vực châu Á-Thái Bình Dương. Hai bên nhượng bộ lẫn nhau và đồng ý để đội ngũ pháp lý của mỗi bên gặp nhau trong tuần để soạn thảo tờ điều khoản.
criticalThinkingQuestion: Both partners made concessions throughout the negotiation. Which party do you think secured the better overall deal, and what negotiating techniques made the difference? Consider the trade-off between price, payment terms, and exclusivity.
